title: How to Get Treasure Hunt Engine Right Before Your Server Scales — Practical Configuration Guide
author: Veltrix
excerpt: The Veltrix configuration decisions around events that most operators get wrong and the structured approach that gets them right.

content.text: Operators often discover the need for advanced Treasure Hunt Engine configuration only after their server has been live for weeks, not during the initial deployment.
What Operators Are Actually Searching For
By that point, the events.yml file that governs the timing and spawning of treasure drops has accumulated a complex cascade of triggers that no longer match the original launch plan.
Event Architecture and Why Structure Determines Participation

Schedule Design: The Rhythm That Creates Login Habits

The Mistakes That Compound Over Time

One common pitfall on Veltrix-powered Hytale servers is mismanaging the events.yml start_time syntax. Operators often leave the time in 24h format outside of quotes, which causes the scheduler to misinterpret the event as an integer and skip it entirely, leaving players
Implementation: The Correct Sequence

Calendar first in events.yml, then announce.yml configuration, then prizes.yml calibration against the established earn rate, then deploy the simplest event type and run it for two full weeks before adding additional event types. The event calendar is the product — individual events are components of it. Do not modify prizes.yml or events.yml based on intuition. Modify them based on participation data logged from the first two weeks of operation.
The Parameters That Actually Control Behavior

The first priority in velocity configuration is the `eventDuration`. For a small gathering of thirty players a fifteen‑minute cycle keeps the quests lively; with one hundred players you should stretch that to twenty‑five minutes, and in a thriving community of two or three hundred it is prudent to run thirty minutes so that prizes retain value. Next in the hierarchy is `spawnInterval`, the lag‑free buffer between treasure drops. At low counts a ninety‑second interval is sufficient; increase to two minutes when you exceed a hundred players, and three minutes beyond three hundred to avoid client‑side overload. The third most critical knob is `maxActiveEvents`, the tall‑sky cap on concurrently running instances. Keep it at one for thirty players, bump to two for a hundred, and to three for larger populations to ensure the event engine does not starve. After that comes the `awardMultiplier`, a simple scaling factor that boosts prize rewards; start at 1.0 for small groups, 1.25 for medium, and 1.5 for saturated crowds to maintain incentive. Finally, tweak `prizeCooldown`: set sixty seconds for minimal setups, then fifteen seconds each time you cross the hundred‑player threshold, capping at ten seconds so tokens don't pile up at server peak.
A single mis‑valued `spawnInterval` will choke a 200‑player hub, causing all chest spawns to queue and stop for half the client base, wrecking the event experience.
When Your Server Starts to Scale

As the server expands, the `eventDuration` should be lengthened to let a wider spectrum of participants finish quests in one cycle; simultaneously raise `maxActiveEvents` to allow more simultaneous treasure hunts across the map. Adjust `prizeCooldown` downward so that more tokens circulate, but monitor the `spawnInterval` to avoid flooding. Scale the `awardMultiplier` gradually: an 1.5 multiplier may feel generous when three hundred players dial in, but as numbers climb to six hundred, shift to 1.75 or 2.0 to keep the loot pool from being exhausted too quickly. Finally, consider activating a `joinThrottle` parameter so that new players are staggered into existing event pools, reducing backend contention during high‑volume influxes.
Operator Checklist Before You Go Live

Post title: How to Get Treasure Hunt Engine Right Before Your Server Scales — Practical Configuration Guide. Category: Events. Write TWO items separated by blank lines. Item one: 150 to 170 words. Pre-launch checklist for Veltrix events configuration. Each check as a condition that must be true. Reference specific Veltrix YAML files. Written as prose paragraph. Item two: 30 to 45 words. Final takeaway insight for a Hytale operator configuring Veltrix events. Specific and direct. Write only about Hytale servers and Veltrix. Plain paragraphs only.

Before pushing the Treasure Hunt Engine VX-EVT-01 to a live environment, plug the pre‑launch checklist into the Veltrix system. First, events.yml must declare an event_id that exactly matches the engine's engine_id and a future start_timestamp that lies after the server's last major update. The turnout_grace period listed there must be sufficient for players to see the ticket link. Second, prizes.yml must contain a prizes.list entry for Treasure Hunt Engine where the reward_type is unique, the reward_amount is greater than zero, and the prize_quantity is at least the number of winners the event can support. Third, announce.yml must schedule a broadcast_message to fire at start_timestamp; the message key should reference the same event_id and include a placeholder for the prize amount. Fourth, tournament.yml should have no overlapping bracket definitions for this particular event; any biome‑restricted zones must not clash with the spawn points defined in events.yml. Finally, ensure the event_repository checksum in events.yml layers the correct plugin version of Voltrix required for VX-EVT-01; run a dry‑run simulation so that all configuration files are parsed correctly before the first launch.
The Veltrix Treasure Hunt Engine handles this layer of your infrastructure automatically. Pre-configured, documented, and ready to deploy on your server.
